content.text: What it is

Data quality remediation workflows are structured processes that convert quality check failures in a RAG knowledge base into actionable, tracked work items — from the moment a freshness, completeness, or accuracy issue is detected through to verification that the issue is resolved and the document is correctly indexed. Rather than leaving failed checks as silent rejections or ad-hoc fixes, a remediation workflow assigns ownership, priority, SLA, and a defined set of permitted remediation actions for each failure type. The workflow closes with a verification step that re-runs the original quality check to confirm the issue is resolved before the ticket is closed and the audit log is updated.
Why it matters

Quality checks are only as valuable as the remediation they trigger. A check that detects stale or incomplete documents but routes failures to an email inbox that nobody monitors provides the appearance of governance without the substance. Poorly managed remediation creates three problems: backlogs of unresolved quality issues accumulate silently; stewards have no visibility into which failures they own or how long they have been outstanding; and auditors have no evidence that detected issues were resolved within acceptable timeframes.

Structured remediation workflows transform quality failures into a measurable backlog with SLAs, owners, and a resolution audit trail — the same disciplines that software teams apply to bug tracking applied to knowledge base governance.
Benefits
SLA enforcement
— each issue type gets a defined resolution SLA (e.g., P1 critical issues resolved within 4 hours, staleness issues within 5 business days), making governance commitments measurable.
Owner accountability
— every issue is assigned to a named steward; escalation paths are triggered automatically when SLAs are breached.
Audit completeness
— the remediation log records who took what action, when, and what the re-verification result was — creating a complete evidence chain from detection to resolution.
Pattern identification

— a structured backlog makes systematic quality problems visible: if 40% of a domain's issues are missing-field failures from a specific SharePoint library, that points to a source system configuration problem, not individual document failures.
Closed-loop quality improvement
— remediation root causes feed back into the ingestion pipeline configuration, quality thresholds, and source system templates, improving upstream data quality over time.
Trade-offs and limitations
Process overhead
— structured workflows add coordination cost. For low-volume, low-stakes knowledge bases, the process overhead may exceed the value of formal tracking.
SLA pressure creating perverse incentives
— if stewards are measured on SLA compliance, there is an incentive to close issues quickly by lowering thresholds rather than actually fixing the underlying document quality.
Triage complexity
— correctly classifying a failure as staleness vs. accuracy vs. completeness requires judgment. Misclassification routes issues to the wrong remediation path and owner.
Remediation tooling investment
— a self-service remediation portal requires engineering investment. Without good tooling, stewards revert to spreadsheet tracking, which defeats the purpose of the workflow.
How it works

The workflow has five stages. Detection: a quality check (freshness, completeness, or accuracy) fails and creates a quarantine record with the document identifier, failure type, failure reason, and severity assessment. Triage: the system or a steward classifies the issue by type and severity. Severity is typically P1 (document actively misleads users), P2 (document incomplete but not actively harmful), or P3 (minor metadata gap). Assignment: the issue is assigned to the domain steward with an SLA timer. Remediation: the steward takes one or more permitted actions depending on the issue type. Verification: the quality check is re-run on the remediated document. If it passes, the issue is closed and the audit log is updated. If it fails, the issue is re-opened with a new SLA.

Permitted remediation actions depend on issue type. Staleness issues: the steward confirms that the document content is still accurate (re-certification) or updates the source document and re-ingests. Completeness issues: the steward enriches missing metadata fields in the source system and triggers re-ingestion. Accuracy issues: the steward corrects the source document content, removes the incorrect chunks from the index, and re-ingests the corrected version. Critical issues: an immediate suspension is triggered (removing chunks from retrieval) before the investigation begins.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Data quality remediation workflow: quality check fails, triage assigns type and severity, three paths for staleness/incompleteness/accuracy plus critical path, all converging to remediation actions, then verification and audit log close.

content.text: The workflow branches by issue type at triage but converges at the remediation actions box. Critical issues bypass triage and go directly to suspension. All paths end with a verification re-run before the ticket is closed. The diagram emphasises that the audit log captures the full chain — detection, classification, action, and re-verification — not just the final state.
When to use it
When a knowledge base receives a significant volume of quality check failures that cannot be managed informally across steward teams.
When audit requirements demand evidence that detected quality issues were resolved within specific timeframes.
When quality failures have previously led to user-facing AI response errors and the organisation needs a closed-loop correction mechanism.
When multiple domains and stewards are involved and informal coordination is creating resolution bottlenecks.
How to implement
Define issue types and SLAs
— document the full set of issue types (staleness, missing field, structural incompleteness, accuracy, duplicate) and assign an SLA to each. Publish these in the governance charter.
Automate issue creation

— quality check failures must automatically create tracked issues in the remediation system (Jira, ServiceNow, or a custom portal) with all diagnostic fields pre-populated. Never require stewards to manually create tickets.
Implement auto-triage for known failure types

— staleness failures (expired max_age) and missing-field failures can be classified and severity-assigned automatically. Reserve human triage for accuracy issues where the nature of the problem requires judgment.
Build self-service remediation actions
— stewards should be able to trigger re-ingestion, metadata update, or document suspension directly from the remediation portal without opening a separate engineering ticket.
Implement verification re-runs
— when a steward marks a remediation as complete, the system must automatically re-run the original quality check on the updated document. The issue cannot be closed until the check passes.
Track remediation metrics
— measure mean time to resolve per issue type and domain, SLA breach rate, re-open rate (issues where the first remediation did not pass verification), and root cause distribution.
Examples

# remediation/issue_manager.py
# Creates and tracks quality remediation issues

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from enum import Enum
import uuid


class IssueType(Enum):
    STALENESS = "staleness"
    MISSING_FIELD = "missing_field"
    SHORT_CONTENT = "short_content"
    ACCURACY = "accuracy"
    DUPLICATE = "duplicate"


class Severity(Enum):
    P1_CRITICAL = "P1"  # SLA: 4 hours — immediate suspension required
    P2_MAJOR = "P2"     # SLA: 2 business days
    P3_MINOR = "P3"     # SLA: 5 business days


# Severity mapping by issue type (can be overridden by triage)
DEFAULT_SEVERITY: dict[IssueType, Severity] = {
    IssueType.STALENESS: Severity.P2_MAJOR,
    IssueType.MISSING_FIELD: Severity.P3_MINOR,
    IssueType.SHORT_CONTENT: Severity.P3_MINOR,
    IssueType.ACCURACY: Severity.P1_CRITICAL,  # assume worst case until investigated
    IssueType.DUPLICATE: Severity.P3_MINOR,
}

SLA_HOURS: dict[Severity, int] = {
    Severity.P1_CRITICAL: 4,
    Severity.P2_MAJOR: 48,   # 2 business days
    Severity.P3_MINOR: 120,  # 5 business days
}


@dataclass
class RemediationIssue:
    issue_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    doc_id: str = ""
    domain: str = ""
    issue_type: IssueType = IssueType.MISSING_FIELD
    severity: Severity = Severity.P3_MINOR
    failure_reason: str = ""
    assigned_steward: str = ""
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc))
    due_at: datetime = field(init=False)
    status: str = "open"  # open | in_progress | pending_verify | resolved | reopened
    resolution_action: str | None = None
    resolved_at: datetime | None = None
    reopen_count: int = 0

    def __post_init__(self):
        hours = SLA_HOURS[self.severity]
        self.due_at = self.created_at + timedelta(hours=hours)

    @property
    def sla_breached(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.status not in ("resolved",)
            and datetime.now(timezone.utc) > self.due_at
        )


def create_issue(
    check_result,  # CheckResult from quality checks
    domain: str,
    steward_email: str,
) -> RemediationIssue:
    """Auto-create a remediation issue from a quality check failure."""
    # Map check_type to IssueType
    type_map = {
        "freshness": IssueType.STALENESS,
        "completeness": IssueType.MISSING_FIELD,
    }
    issue_type = type_map.get(check_result.check_type, IssueType.MISSING_FIELD)
    severity = DEFAULT_SEVERITY[issue_type]

    return RemediationIssue(
        doc_id=check_result.doc_id,
        domain=domain,
        issue_type=issue_type,
        severity=severity,
        failure_reason="; ".join(check_result.failure_reasons),
        assigned_steward=steward_email,
    )

# remediation/actions.py
# Permitted remediation actions per issue type

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Callable


def re_ingest_document(
    doc_id: str,
    ingestion_pipeline: object,  # your ingestion client
    audit_log: object,
    actor: str,
) -> dict:
    """Re-submit a document to the ingestion pipeline after source update."""
    result = ingestion_pipeline.submit(doc_id)
    audit_log.write({
        "action": "re_ingest",
        "doc_id": doc_id,
        "actor": actor,
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "pipeline_run_id": result.get("run_id"),
    })
    return result


def suspend_document(
    doc_id: str,
    vector_store: object,
    audit_log: object,
    actor: str,
    reason: str,
) -> None:
    """Immediately suspend a document from retrieval (P1 critical path)."""
    vector_store.set_approved(doc_id, approved=False)
    audit_log.write({
        "action": "suspend",
        "doc_id": doc_id,
        "actor": actor,
        "reason": reason,
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    })


def verify_and_close(
    issue: object,
    quality_checker: Callable,
    document: dict,
    audit_log: object,
    actor: str,
) -> bool:
    """Re-run the original check. Close if passing; reopen if still failing."""
    result = quality_checker(document)
    if result.passed:
        issue.status = "resolved"
        issue.resolved_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        issue.resolution_action = actor
        audit_log.write({"action": "close", "issue_id": issue.issue_id,
                         "actor": actor, "timestamp": issue.resolved_at.isoformat()})
        return True
    else:
        issue.status = "reopened"
        issue.reopen_count += 1
        audit_log.write({"action": "reopen", "issue_id": issue.issue_id,
                         "reason": result.failure_reasons,
                         "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()})
        return False
Real-world example

A large professional services firm had deployed an AI assistant for its legal team that drew from a knowledge base of 4,000 internal legal memos, regulatory summaries, and client guidance documents. The quality check system was detecting roughly 120 failures per week — but all failures were being sent to a shared email alias. After six weeks, 650 unresolved issues had accumulated, the quality backlog was unknown to leadership, and three AI response errors traced back to stale documents that had been flagged weeks earlier but not remediated.

The firm replaced the email-alias approach with a Jira Service Management workflow. Quality check failures auto-created Jira tickets with pre-populated fields: document title, domain, failure type, severity, assigned steward, and a direct link to the remediation portal. P1 issues triggered an immediate PagerDuty alert. Stewards were given a self-service portal that let them re-ingest, suspend, or enrich metadata directly from the ticket — eliminating the need to open a separate engineering request.

Within eight weeks, the backlog was cleared. Mean time to resolve dropped from 23 days (estimated average before the workflow) to 4.2 days for P2 issues and 2.1 hours for P1 issues. The re-open rate (issues where the first remediation did not pass verification) was 8%, pointing to stewards updating metadata in the wrong system — a process gap that was fixed by connecting the metadata form in the remediation portal directly to the authoritative document management system.
Common mistakes
Manual issue creation
— requiring stewards to manually create tickets from detection alerts introduces lag and errors. Issue creation must be automated from the quality check failure event.
No verification step
— closing an issue because a steward says they fixed it, without re-running the quality check, creates a false resolution record. Verification must be automated.
Assigning all failures to the same steward
— a shared inbox or shared steward for all domains creates a bottleneck. Issue assignment must route to the document's domain steward.
No SLA escalation
— SLAs without escalation are advisory. When a P1 issue breaches its 4-hour SLA, the deputy steward and governance lead must be automatically notified.
Treating all failures as equal

— a missing-author field and a document that gives dangerously incorrect medical advice are not the same. Severity classification determines urgency; without it, everything is treated as medium priority.
Best practices
Auto-classify issue type and severity from the quality check failure reason
— most failure types have a well-defined mapping to severity. Reserve human triage for accuracy issues where the content problem requires judgment.
Gate ticket closure on automated verification
— the remediation system must not allow a ticket to be moved to 'resolved' status without a passing re-run of the original quality check.
Publish a weekly remediation health report
— open issue count by domain and severity, SLA breach rate, and mean time to resolve are leading indicators. Surface them to AI governance leadership before they manifest as user incidents.
Analyse root causes monthly
— aggregate remediation data to identify systemic sources of failure (e.g., a specific SharePoint library that consistently produces missing-field failures) and address the root cause upstream.
Treat re-open rate as a process quality metric

— a high re-open rate (issues where the first remediation action did not resolve the quality failure) indicates that either the remediation action is insufficient or the quality check threshold is miscalibrated.
Performance and scalability

Remediation workflows are people-limited, not compute-limited. The throughput bottleneck is steward capacity. A well-instrumented system will expose this through the SLA breach rate — when breach rate rises, it indicates either too many issues for the steward's available time or too many of the issues are time-intensive accuracy problems. Both signals are actionable: the first points to steward capacity; the second points to upstream source quality problems that are generating complex remediations.

For high-volume knowledge bases, AI-assisted triage can reduce the manual burden: an LLM pre-screening accuracy issues to distinguish plausible-but-outdated content from content that is factually incorrect reduces the cognitive load on stewards reviewing complex documents.

Enterprise ITSM workflow with SLA enforcement
Preferred in large enterprise; stronger SLA dashboard support

P1 issue escalation and on-call routing
Integrates with Jira for automated escalation on SLA breach

Data quality issue management within governance catalogue
Native integration with steward portal and lineage

Security and reliability

The remediation system must not allow issues to be silently deleted or closed without verification. Write access to the issue store should be restricted to the quality check pipeline (create), the remediation portal (update status, record action), and the verification job (close on passing check). The audit log that records each status transition must be append-only and separate from the mutable issue record.
Append-only audit log for issue transitions
— every status change (open → in_progress, in_progress → pending_verify, pending_verify → resolved/reopened) must be logged with actor and timestamp.
No direct delete of issues
— issues that are determined to be false positives should be closed with a 'false_positive' reason, not deleted. Regulators may query historical issue counts.
P1 suspension must not require human approval
— documents classified as P1 must be suspended immediately from retrieval on detection, before triage is complete. Human approval loops on P1 suspensions introduce unacceptable risk windows.
Key takeaways
Quality checks without structured remediation produce governance theatre — failures are detected but not resolved, creating a false sense of control.
Issue creation must be fully automated from the quality check failure event; manual ticket creation introduces lag and errors that degrade the remediation SLA.
Verification re-runs are mandatory before closing any issue; a 'fixed' status based only on the steward's assertion, not a passing quality check, is not a valid closure.
P1 critical issues must trigger immediate automatic suspension from retrieval — no human approval loop — with investigation to follow.
Remediation metrics (MTTR, SLA breach rate, re-open rate, root cause distribution) are the primary operational health indicators for a knowledge base governance programme.
Root cause analysis on remediation data identifies systemic upstream quality problems that individual issue resolution cannot fix.
